workout.title: Chest Day - BODYBUILD
workout.description: Chest Day workout focusing on bodybuild training.

exercises.name: Barbell Wide Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set up a flat bench under a rack and load the barbell; position yourself lying supine on the bench with eyes roughly under the bar.
instructions: Set up a flat bench under a rack and load the barbell; position yourself lying supine on the bench with eyes roughly under the bar.
Plant feet firmly on the floor, pull your shoulder blades together and down (retract and depress) and maintain a tight core and neutral spine.
Grip the bar with a wide, pronated grip (wider than shoulder-width) so the forearms are more angled when the bar is at chest level.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar over your chest, keeping shoulders stable and the elbows under the bar.
Inhale, slowly lower the bar in a controlled manner to the mid-chest / nipple line while keeping the elbows tracking out at a moderate flare and the scapulae retracted.
Pause briefly with the bar lightly touching the chest (do not bounce), then exhale and press the bar back up along a slight arc until the arms are nearly extended.
Avoid locking the elbows aggressively at the top, maintain tension in the pectorals and triceps, and keep the wrists neutral.
Return the bar to the rack with control after your set; use a spotter if lifting heavy or approaching failure.
exercises.description: A barbell bench press performed with a wider-than-shoulder grip to emphasize the outer pectoral fibers. Lying supine on a flat bench, the bar is lowered to the mid-chest and pressed back to full arm extension.

exercises.name: Barbell Incline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set an adjustable bench to a 30-45° incline and position it inside a power rack or with a spotter and place the barbell on the rack at a height you can reach while seated.
instructions: Set an adjustable bench to a 30-45° incline and position it inside a power rack or with a spotter and place the barbell on the rack at a height you can reach while seated.
Sit with your back and head firmly against the bench, feet flat on the floor, and brace your core (tighten abs and glutes) to maintain a stable, slight arch in the lower back.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists stacked over elbows and shoulders pulled down and back.
Unrack the bar by straightening your arms and moving the bar directly above your upper chest/shoulder line; have a spotter if needed.
Inhale, bend the elbows, and lower the bar under control to the upper chest/clavicle area (touch point just below the top of the sternum) keeping elbows at roughly 30–60° from the torso.
Pause briefly at the bottom without bouncing, ensuring the forearms remain vertical and the core stays braced.
Exhale and press the bar back up in a slightly backward arc until the arms are fully extended but not locked aggressively at the elbows.
Repeat for the desired repetitions, then carefully re-rack the bar on the uprights with control or have the spotter assist.
exercises.description: The barbell incline bench press is a compound pressing movement performed on an inclined bench that emphasizes the upper portion of the pectorals while also engaging the anterior delts and triceps. It is done from a supine position on an incline bench, pressing a barbell vertically from chest to full arm extension.

exercises.name: Cable Bent Over Single Arm Crossover
exercises.notes: Set a single handle on a cable pulley at about shoulder to chest height and select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Set a single handle on a cable pulley at about shoulder to chest height and select an appropriate weight.

Stand to the side of the pulley with a staggered or shoulder-width stance and hinge forward at the hips until your torso is roughly 30–45° from vertical (slight bend in knees); maintain a neutral spine and brace your abs.
Grab the handle with the hand farthest from the machine, arm extended out to the side and palm facing down or slightly inward; allow a soft bend in the elbow.

Keep your shoulder down and stable, then exhale and pull the handle in a horizontal arc across your body toward the center of your chest, leading with the elbow and squeezing the pectorals at the end range.
Pause briefly with the chest contracted, keeping the torso still (avoid rotating the ribs or shoulders), then inhale and slowly reverse the motion to return the hand to the start under control.
Perform the desired reps, then switch sides and repeat. Maintain wrist neutrality and controlled tempo throughout.
exercises.description: A single-arm cable movement performed from a bent-over stance that sweeps the working arm across the body to load and compress the chest fibers, emphasizing the inner and upper pectoral region while the torso is stabilized.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

workout.title: Back Day - BODYBUILD
workout.description: Back Day workout focusing on bodybuild training.

exercises.name: Barbell Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly 45 degrees to parallel to the floor; keep the spine neutral (no rounding) and engage the abs and glutes.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and the barbell over mid-foot; use an overhand (pronated) grip slightly wider than shoulder width.
Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly 45 degrees to parallel to the floor; keep the spine neutral (no rounding) and engage the abs and glutes.
Hold the bar with arms extended and shoulders protracted, chest up and eyes looking a few feet in front of you to maintain a neutral neck.
Initiate the rep by driving the elbows back and up, pulling the bar toward your lower chest / upper abdomen while squeezing the shoulder blades together.
Keep the elbows close to the body (not flaring excessively) and avoid using momentum from the hips; breathe out as you pull.
Lower the bar slowly and under control to the starting position with arms extended while maintaining the hip hinge and neutral spine; breathe in on the descent.
Repeat for the desired reps, controlling tempo and keeping the torso stable — if you cannot maintain a flat back, reduce weight or stop and reset.
exercises.description: Standing hip-hinged barbell row where you pull a loaded barbell toward the lower chest/upper abdomen; primarily targets the lats and upper back while requiring posterior chain and core stabilization. Maintain a flat back and drive the elbows back to retract the shoulder blades.

exercises.name: Cable Seated Row with V-bar
exercises.notes: Attach the V-bar to the low pulley and grasp it with a neutral grip (palms facing each other).
instructions: Sit on the bench facing the cable machine with feet braced against the footplate and knees slightly bent.
Attach the V-bar to the low pulley and grasp it with a neutral grip (palms facing each other). Sit upright with a neutral spine and chest lifted.
Start with your arms extended and shoulders relaxed forward — feel a slight stretch in the lats.
Exhale and initiate the movement by driving your elbows straight back, keeping them close to your sides.
Retract and squeeze the shoulder blades together and pull the V-bar to the lower chest/upper abdomen; avoid excessive torso lean or using momentum.
Hold and squeeze the contracted position for 1 second, focusing on the lats and upper back.
Inhale and slowly extend your arms forward under control until the lats are stretched and you return to the start position.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, maintaining a neutral spine and controlled tempo throughout.
exercises.description: Seated cable row with a V-bar is a horizontal pulling exercise that emphasizes the lats and middle back by pulling the handle toward the torso while seated. It uses a neutral grip to allow a strong elbow-driven pull and a full contraction of the back muscles.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bent Over Reverse Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is leaned forward (torso roughly parallel or at a 30–45° angle depending on flexibility), keeping the spine neutral and neck aligned.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart holding a dumbbell in each hand with a reverse (palms-up/supinated) grip.

Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is leaned forward (torso roughly parallel or at a 30–45° angle depending on flexibility), keeping the spine neutral and neck aligned.
Let the arms hang straight toward the floor with the dumbbells under your shoulders; brace your abs to stabilize the spine.
Initiate the row by pulling the dumbbells up and back toward your lower ribs/waist, driving the elbows close to your body and squeezing the shoulder blades together.
Pause briefly at the top with scapular retraction, keeping the torso still and avoiding excessive back extension or shrugging.
Slowly lower the dumbbells back to the start under control until the arms are nearly extended, maintaining the hip hinge and neutral spine.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, breathing out as you pull and inhaling as you lower.
exercises.description: A bent-over reverse-grip dumbbell row performed from a hip-hinged position to emphasize the upper back and rear shoulder muscles while using a supinated (reverse) grip. The movement pulls the weights toward the torso, engaging the scapular retractors and arm flexors.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

workout.title: Shoulder Day - BODYBUILD
workout.description: Shoulder Day workout focusing on bodybuild training.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Alternate Overhead Press
exercises.notes: Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart, knees slightly soft and core braced; hold a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder level (front-rack position) with palms facing forward or slightly inward.
instructions: Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart, knees slightly soft and core braced; hold a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder level (front-rack position) with palms facing forward or slightly inward.
Keep the wrist neutral and elbow beneath the dumbbell, scapulae stable and chest up; inhale to prepare and engage the abs.
Exhale and press one dumbbell straight overhead by extending the elbow and driving through the shoulder until the arm is fully extended overhead without arching the lower back.
Pause briefly at the top with the arm locked out and the dumbbell balanced over the shoulder/hip line, then control the descent back to the shoulder (front-rack) position.
As you lower the pressed arm, immediately repeat the press with the opposite arm so you alternate sides each rep; keep the non-pressing arm stable at rack height to help balance.
Continue alternating reps while maintaining a neutral spine, tight abs, and minimal torso rotation; avoid using leg drive or leaning back to lift heavier weight.
Perform repetitions in a controlled tempo and choose a weight that allows full range of motion while keeping proper form.
exercises.description: A standing single-arm (alternating) dumbbell press that develops shoulder strength and stability by pressing one weight overhead at a time while the other arm remains at rack position. Maintain a tight core and neutral spine to control the movement and prevent torso lean.

exercises.name: Lever Seated Shoulder Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on the machine with your back fully against the pad and feet flat on the floor, knees bent at ~90 degrees.
instructions: Sit upright on the machine with your back fully against the pad and feet flat on the floor, knees bent at ~90 degrees.
Adjust the seat height so the handles are at or just below shoulder level when your elbows are bent.
Grip the handles with a neutral or overhand grip, wrists in line with forearms, and elbows pointed slightly forward (not flared straight out).
Brace your core and keep your head and upper back pressed against the pad throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the handles upward in a controlled arc until your arms are nearly extended; avoid locking the elbows at the top.
Inhale and lower the handles slowly back to the start position with control until your elbows are at approximately shoulder level.
Use a weight that allows smooth, controlled reps without excessive back arching; stop the set if you cannot maintain form.
exercises.description: A seated machine (lever) shoulder press where you push handles upward from shoulder level to work the delts. The machine stabilizes the path while triceps and upper back/traps assist and stabilize the movement.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Reverse Fly
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30-45 degrees and position it so you can lie face down with your chest supported and feet on the floor or toes braced behind you (prone on bench).
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30-45 degrees and position it so you can lie face down with your chest supported and feet on the floor or toes braced behind you (prone on bench).
Pick up a pair of light-to-moderate dumbbells and hold them with a neutral grip (palms facing each other) with arms hanging straight down beneath your shoulders.
Tuck your chin slightly so your neck stays neutral and pull your shoulder blades down and back (scapular retraction) before starting the rep.

With a slight bend in the elbows, raise the dumbbells out to your sides in a wide arc until your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor; lead the movement from the shoulders (posterior delts), not the hands.
Pause and squeeze the shoulder blades and traps at the top for 1 second, keeping the torso stable on the bench and avoiding trunk rotation or swinging.
Lower the dumbbells slowly and under control back to the starting position, maintaining the slight elbow bend and scapular control.
Repeat for the desired reps, breathing out as you lift and in as you lower. Reduce weight if you must use momentum to complete the reps.
exercises.description: Chest-supported dumbbell reverse fly performed prone on an incline bench that isolates the posterior delts while also engaging the upper back and traps for scapular retraction. Use a controlled, wide-arc motion with a slight elbow bend to avoid momentum.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

workout.description: Arm Day workout focusing on bodybuild training.

exercises.name: Barbell Incline Close Grip Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and load the barbell safely; position a spotter if using heavy weight.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and load the barbell safely; position a spotter if using heavy weight.
Lie supine on the incline bench with feet flat on the floor and your eyes roughly under the bar; retract and pinch your shoulder blades together.
Grip the bar with a close (shoulder-width or slightly narrower) overhand grip, thumbs wrapped around the bar.
Unrack the bar by extending your arms and moving the bar out over your upper chest; lock your shoulder blades and keep your spine neutral.
Slowly lower the bar in a controlled path to the upper chest/clavicle area, keeping your elbows tucked toward your sides (about 30–45 degrees from the torso).
Pause briefly with the bar just touching or an inch above the chest, maintaining tight core and shoulder position.
Press the bar upward by driving through the triceps and extending the elbows until arms are nearly straight; avoid aggressive elbow hyperextension.
Repeat for desired reps, then carefully rack the bar with control; breathe in on the descent and exhale as you press up.
exercises.description: A close-grip barbell press performed on an incline bench that emphasizes the triceps while also working the upper pectorals and anterior delts. The narrower hand placement and bench angle shift load from the chest to the arms and shoulders.

exercises.name: Cable Triceps Pushdown (V bar)
exercises.notes: Set the cable pulley to the high position and attach a V‑bar.
instructions: Set the cable pulley to the high position and attach a V‑bar. Select an appropriate weight that allows controlled reps.
Stand facing the machine with feet hip‑width apart and a slight stagger if preferred for balance; grasp the V‑bar with a neutral (palms facing each other) grip.
Start with your elbows bent ~90°, upper arms close to your sides, and a slight forward lean at the hips so your torso is stable and shoulders are over your hips.
Brace your core and keep your upper arms stationary; exhale and press the V‑bar down by extending your elbows until your arms are fully straight but not locked aggressively.
Squeeze the triceps at the bottom of the movement for a brief pause (1 second) while maintaining neutral wrists.
Inhale and slowly return the V‑bar to the starting position under control, allowing the elbows to bend until the forearms are roughly parallel to the floor or reach the chosen start depth.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions while keeping the torso still and avoiding use of momentum.
Common form cues: keep elbows tucked to your sides (do not let them drift forward or flare out), maintain neutral wrists, and avoid leaning back or swinging the body to complete reps.
exercises.description: Standing cable triceps pushdown using a V‑bar targets the triceps through elbow extension while the V‑grip emphasizes the medial head and allows a neutral wrist position. Performed from a high pulley, the movement is a strict elbow hinge with minimal shoulder movement.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Alternate Biceps Curl
exercises.notes: Stand upright with feet hip-width apart, knees slightly bent, core braced and back neutral; hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with palms facing forward (supinated grip).
instructions: Stand upright with feet hip-width apart, knees slightly bent, core braced and back neutral; hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with palms facing forward (supinated grip).
Keep your elbows close to your torso and stationary throughout the movement; do not let them drift forward or backward.
Exhale and curl the right dumbbell by flexing the elbow until the dumbbell approaches shoulder level; optionally rotate the wrist slightly so the palm faces the shoulder at the top.
Pause and squeeze the biceps at the top for about 1 second without leaning back or using body momentum.
Inhale and lower the right dumbbell slowly and under control back to the starting position until the elbow is nearly extended (do not hyperextend).
Repeat the same curling motion with the left arm, alternating arms each rep while the opposite arm remains extended at your side.
Maintain a steady, controlled tempo (avoid swinging the torso) and choose a weight that allows full range of motion with good form for all reps.
exercises.description: A standing alternating dumbbell curl that isolates the biceps by lifting one arm at a time while the opposite arm remains extended; the forearms assist with grip and elbow flexion. Performed with controlled tempo to limit shoulder involvement and momentum.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Lever Linear Hack Squat
exercises.notes: Setup: Sit on the hack-squat machine with your back and shoulders firmly against the pads (or hold the handles).
instructions: Setup: Sit on the hack-squat machine with your back and shoulders firmly against the pads (or hold the handles). Place your feet about shoulder-width apart on the lower portion of the angled footplate with toes slightly turned out and heels flat.
Unlock: Secure your grip on the handles and disengage the machine safety/locking mechanism so the sled can move freely.
Brace: Tighten your core, keep your chest up and spine neutral, and ensure knees are tracking in line with your toes.

Descent: Inhale and slowly lower the sled by bending your knees and hips, keeping your back pressed to the pad. Descend until your thighs are roughly parallel to the footplate or about 90 degrees at the knees (stop earlier if you feel discomfort).
Drive: Exhale and push through your heels to extend your knees and hips, driving the sled back up to the starting position. Do not hyperextend or lock the knees forcefully at the top.
Control and repeat: Maintain a controlled tempo on both the descent and ascent, keeping heels down and knees aligned. After finishing your set, re-engage the safety/lock before exiting the machine.
exercises.description: A machine hack-squat variation performed on a linear/lever hack machine that emphasizes knee extension to target the quadriceps while the sled moves on an angled path. The exercise uses a back/seat support and footplate so you push the sled with the feet to extend the knees and hips.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Rear Lunge from Step
exercises.notes: Start standing near the front edge of a step or aerobic platform with your front foot placed flat on the step and the rear foot on the floor behind you; hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides, arms straight.
instructions: Start standing near the front edge of a step or aerobic platform with your front foot placed flat on the step and the rear foot on the floor behind you; hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides, arms straight.
Stand tall with chest up, shoulders back, core engaged and feet hip-width apart for balance before you step back.

Keeping your torso upright, step the rear foot straight back off the step and lower your hips until the front thigh is about parallel to the step (front knee roughly 90°) and the back knee is close to the floor.
Make sure the front knee tracks over the toes and does not collapse inward; maintain evenly distributed weight through the front heel and midfoot.
Pause briefly at the bottom while keeping core braced, then press through the front heel to drive your body upward and return the rear foot back up onto the step to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired reps on one leg, then switch legs so the other foot starts on the step. Use slow, controlled movements and breathe out when driving up, inhale as you lower.
exercises.description: A unilateral lower-body exercise performed from a raised step where you step the rear leg back into a lunge while holding dumbbells. It emphasizes the front thigh (quadriceps) while also recruiting the glutes, hamstrings and stabilizers for balance.

exercises.name: Barbell Seated Calf Raise
exercises.notes: Sit upright near the edge of a flat bench with knees bent at about 90 degrees and feet flat on the floor (or on a small block for increased range of motion).
instructions: Sit upright near the edge of a flat bench with knees bent at about 90 degrees and feet flat on the floor (or on a small block for increased range of motion).
Place a barbell across your upper thighs just above the knees; use a pad if needed for comfort and grip the bar with both hands to steady it (seated position).
Position the balls of your feet on the floor (or a small raised platform) while keeping heels hanging or supported so your ankles can move through full range.
Keeping your torso upright and core engaged, exhale and press through the balls of your feet to raise your heels as high as possible, squeezing the calves at the top.

Pause briefly at the top with the calves fully contracted, then inhale and slowly lower the heels back down until you feel a stretch in the calves but maintain control (avoid letting the weight slam down).
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions; maintain a steady tempo, avoid bouncing, and keep knees stationary throughout the set.
To stop, carefully roll the barbell forward off your thighs or have a spotter help lift it off before standing.
exercises.description: Seated on a bench with a loaded barbell resting across the thighs, raise the heels by plantarflexing the ankles to target the calf muscles. The movement is performed with knees bent, emphasizing the soleus portion of the calves through controlled heel raises and lowers.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Squat
exercises.notes: Set the bar in a squat rack at about mid-chest height.
instructions: Set the bar in a squat rack at about mid-chest height. Position yourself under the bar so it rests across your upper traps (high-bar) or rear delts (low-bar) depending on preference.
Grip the bar with hands just outside shoulder width, elbows pointing down and chest up. Stand with feet roughly shoulder-width apart and toes pointed slightly outward.
Brace your core and take a full breath in, then unrack the bar by extending your legs and taking 1–2 controlled steps backward to clear the rack; establish a stable, shoulder-width stance.
Keep a neutral spine and an upright chest. Initiate the movement by pushing your hips back slightly and bending at the knees, sending your knees outward to track over your toes.
Descend in a controlled manner until your thighs are at least parallel to the floor (or deeper if mobility allows) while keeping weight on your midfoot/heels and maintaining a braced core.
Avoid knee collapse (valgus) — consciously push knees outward and keep toes engaged. Do not let the lower back round; maintain a neutral spine throughout.
Drive through your heels and midfoot to stand back up, exhaling as you ascend and extending hips and knees simultaneously until fully upright.
Reset your breathing and bracing between reps as needed. When finished, step forward under control and re-rack the bar securely.
Use appropriate weight, a spotter or safety pins in the rack if lifting near maximum loads, and adjust stance or bar position to reduce discomfort in shoulders or lower back.
exercises.description: Barbell back squat is a compound, standing lower-body exercise that primarily develops the quadriceps while also engaging the glutes, hamstrings and core for stability. The barbell rests across the upper back while you descend by bending hips and knees, then drive back up to standing.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Goblet Squat
exercises.notes: Stand upright with feet about shoulder-width apart and toes slightly turned out, holding a single dumbbell vertically at chest level (both hands cupping one end) — this is the goblet position.
instructions: Stand upright with feet about shoulder-width apart and toes slightly turned out, holding a single dumbbell vertically at chest level (both hands cupping one end) — this is the goblet position.
Brace your core and keep a neutral spine and chest lifted; elbows should point down between your knees as you move.
Initiate the movement by sending hips back and bending the knees, keeping weight distributed through the heels and mid-foot; push knees out in line with toes to avoid collapse.
Descend until your thighs are approximately parallel to the floor (or deeper if mobility allows) while maintaining an upright torso and the dumbbell close to your chest.
Pause briefly at the bottom, then push through the heels to extend the hips and knees and return to standing, keeping the core engaged and the dumbbell steady.
Repeat for the desired reps; inhale on the way down and exhale as you drive up. Avoid letting the heels rise or knees cave inward.
exercises.description: A goblet squat performed standing while holding a dumbbell at the chest; it emphasizes knee-driven squat movement to load the quadriceps while also engaging the glutes, hamstrings and core for stability.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Calf Raise
exercises.notes: Stand on the edge of a step or aerobic platform with the balls of both feet on the edge and heels hanging off; feet about hip-width apart.
instructions: Stand on the edge of a step or aerobic platform with the balls of both feet on the edge and heels hanging off; feet about hip-width apart.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with a neutral grip, arms relaxed; optionally lightly touch a wall or railing for balance (wall-assisted if needed).
Keep the torso upright, chest lifted and gaze forward; maintain a slight bend in the knees (do not lock them).
Inhale, then exhale and contract your calves to raise your heels as high as possible until you are standing on your toes; keep weight evenly distributed across the forefoot.
Pause at the top for 1–2 seconds, focusing on a full contraction of the calves while maintaining core tension.
Inhale and slowly lower your heels below the level of the step until you feel a controlled stretch in the calves.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, controlling both the concentric (raise) and eccentric (lower) phases and breathing rhythmically.
exercises.description: A weighted standing calf raise performed on the edge of a step to load the calf muscles through a full plantarflexion and stretch. Dumbbells add resistance and grip demand while the core and glutes stabilize the body.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

workout.description: Upper Body workout focusing on strength training.

exercises.name: Cable Lateral Pulldown (with rope attachment)
exercises.notes: Attach a rope to the high pulley and select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Attach a rope to the high pulley and select an appropriate weight.
Sit on the bench with feet flat on the floor and knees secured under the thigh pads so you cannot rise during the pull.
Grasp the ends of the rope with a neutral (palms facing each other) grip and begin with your arms fully extended overhead, torso upright and a slight natural arch in the lower back.
Initiate the movement by pulling your shoulder blades down and back (scapular depression and retraction) to engage the lats before the elbows move.
Drive your elbows down and out to the sides while pulling the rope toward the upper chest/upper sternum area, separating the rope ends slightly as you reach the bottom of the movement.
Pause and squeeze the lats and upper back at the bottom for about 1 second while keeping the torso stable (avoid excessive leaning back or using momentum).
Slowly return the rope to the start under control by allowing the arms to extend overhead while maintaining tension in the lats.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining a controlled tempo and focusing on elbow-driven movement rather than pulling with the hands.
exercises.description: A seated high-pulley rope pulldown that emphasizes the latissimus dorsi by pulling the rope down and slightly out while keeping the elbows wide. Performed with a neutral/hammer grip to also engage the biceps, rear delts and upper back musculature.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Fly
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30-45 degrees and sit with a dumbbell in each hand resting on your thighs.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30-45 degrees and sit with a dumbbell in each hand resting on your thighs.
Lie back (supine on an incline bench) and plant your feet flat on the floor for stability; bring the dumbbells up to shoulder height with palms facing each other and a slight bend in the elbows.
Retract and depress your shoulder blades slightly (pinch the shoulder blades together) to stabilize the chest and protect the shoulders.
Begin the repetition by slowly lowering the dumbbells out to your sides in a wide arc, keeping the same slight elbow bend throughout; lower until you feel a comfortable stretch in the upper chest.
Keep the movement controlled and avoid dropping the weights below the plane of the chest or allowing the elbows to fully lock or flare excessively.
Drive the dumbbells back up by horizontally adducting the shoulders (bringing the arms together) until they meet or nearly meet above the sternum; exhale as you lift.
Pause briefly at the top with tension in the pectorals, then inhale and repeat for the desired reps.
Maintain a neutral neck and avoid shrugging the shoulders; use a weight that allows strict form and full control through both eccentric and concentric phases.
exercises.description: Performed lying back on an inclined bench, the dumbbell incline fly isolates the upper portion of the chest by moving the arms in a wide arc from open to together while maintaining a slight elbow bend. It emphasizes pectoral horizontal adduction with shoulder stabilization from the delts and grip support from the forearms.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Prioritize form over weight - quality reps build strength.

exercises.name: Barbell Squat
exercises.notes: Set the bar in a squat rack at about mid-chest height.
instructions: Set the bar in a squat rack at about mid-chest height. Position yourself under the bar so it rests across your upper traps (high-bar) or rear delts (low-bar) depending on preference.
Grip the bar with hands just outside shoulder width, elbows pointing down and chest up. Stand with feet roughly shoulder-width apart and toes pointed slightly outward.
Brace your core and take a full breath in, then unrack the bar by extending your legs and taking 1–2 controlled steps backward to clear the rack; establish a stable, shoulder-width stance.
Keep a neutral spine and an upright chest. Initiate the movement by pushing your hips back slightly and bending at the knees, sending your knees outward to track over your toes.
Descend in a controlled manner until your thighs are at least parallel to the floor (or deeper if mobility allows) while keeping weight on your midfoot/heels and maintaining a braced core.
Avoid knee collapse (valgus) — consciously push knees outward and keep toes engaged. Do not let the lower back round; maintain a neutral spine throughout.
Drive through your heels and midfoot to stand back up, exhaling as you ascend and extending hips and knees simultaneously until fully upright.
Reset your breathing and bracing between reps as needed. When finished, step forward under control and re-rack the bar securely.
Use appropriate weight, a spotter or safety pins in the rack if lifting near maximum loads, and adjust stance or bar position to reduce discomfort in shoulders or lower back.
exercises.description: Barbell back squat is a compound, standing lower-body exercise that primarily develops the quadriceps while also engaging the glutes, hamstrings and core for stability. The barbell rests across the upper back while you descend by bending hips and knees, then drive back up to standing.

exercises.name: Landmine Rear Lunge
exercises.notes: Setup: Secure one end of the barbell in a landmine attachment.
instructions: Setup: Secure one end of the barbell in a landmine attachment. Stand facing the bar with feet about hip-width apart and the working leg slightly forward. Grasp the free end of the bar with both hands close to the chest in a goblet-style hold (elbows tucked).
Brace your core and stand tall with a neutral spine and shoulders down and back. Keep weight distributed through the midfoot and heel of the front foot.

Step the non-working leg straight back onto the toes (standing on the ball of the back foot) and lower into a controlled rear lunge by bending both knees until the front thigh is roughly parallel to the floor or the back knee approaches the ground.

Keep the torso upright (slight forward lean allowed), chest proud, and the front knee tracking over the toes without collapsing inward. Maintain a firm grip on the bar against the chest throughout the movement.
Drive through the front heel and contract the glutes to push yourself back up to the starting standing position while bringing the back foot forward.
Complete the desired reps on one leg, then switch legs. Breathe in while lowering and exhale while driving back up.

Coaching tips: Use a controlled tempo, avoid letting the front knee travel excessively past the toes, and reduce weight if balance or form breaks down. Keep the elbow position stable to avoid undue shoulder strain.
exercises.description: A unilateral loaded lunge performed with a landmine bar held at the chest that emphasizes hip extension to drive the back leg and load the glutes while the front leg controls descent and ascent. It challenges single-leg strength, balance, and core stability.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Prioritize form over weight - quality reps build strength.

exercises.name: Barbell Decline Pullover
exercises.notes: Set a decline bench to a moderate angle and secure your feet under the pads so your body is stable and supine on the bench.
instructions: Set a decline bench to a moderate angle and secure your feet under the pads so your body is stable and supine on the bench.
Grasp a barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip about shoulder-width apart and lift it straight over your chest with elbows slightly bent.
Brace your core and keep your hips down to maintain a neutral spine throughout the movement.
Inhale and slowly lower the barbell in a controlled arc behind your head by hinging at the shoulders; keep a slight bend in the elbows and do not let them flare excessively.
Lower until you feel a deep stretch in the chest/lats or until the bar is roughly level with the crown of your head — do not go so far that your lower back overarches.
Exhale and pull the barbell back in the same arc to the start position over your chest by contracting the pectorals and lats; keep the movement smooth and controlled.
Repeat for the desired reps, maintaining steady tempo and full control on both lowering and lifting phases.
Use a spotter or lighter weight if you cannot control the bar safely behind your head; stop if you feel sharp shoulder or back pain.
exercises.description: Lying supine on a decline bench, the barbell pullover moves the weight in an arc from over the chest to behind the head, emphasizing a deep stretch and contraction of the chest while also engaging the lats and triceps.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Press Squat
exercises.notes: Start standing with feet shoulder-width apart, toes pointing slightly outward and a dumbbell held vertically at chest in a goblet grip (hands around the head/handle), elbows close to your body.
instructions: Start standing with feet shoulder-width apart, toes pointing slightly outward and a dumbbell held vertically at chest in a goblet grip (hands around the head/handle), elbows close to your body.
Brace your core, pull your shoulders down and back, keep chest up and eyes forward throughout the movement.
Inhale and hinge at the hips while bending the knees to lower into a squat — push hips back and let knees track over toes until thighs are at least parallel to the floor.
Drive through your heels and midfoot to stand back up, exhaling as you rise and maintaining a strong upright torso.
Once standing, press the dumbbell overhead (or slightly forward depending on comfort), extending the elbows and engaging delts/pectorals; exhale during the press.
Slowly lower the dumbbell back to the goblet position at your chest with control, resetting your brace before the next descent.
Repeat for the desired reps, keeping movements steady, knees tracking toes and avoiding rounded lower back or knees collapsing inward.
exercises.description: A standing compound movement that combines a goblet-style squat with a dumbbell press; it primarily works the quads while also engaging the chest, shoulders and arms. Perform by squatting from a goblet hold and then pressing the weight as you stand.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Prioritize form over weight - quality reps build strength.

exercises.name: Cable Pushdown (straight arm)
exercises.notes: Attach a straight bar, rope, or single handle to a high pulley. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Attach a straight bar, rope, or single handle to a high pulley. Select a moderate weight that allows controlled movement while keeping arms mostly straight.
Stand facing the machine with feet hip-width apart (or a slight stagger for balance). Grasp the handle with both hands (pronated or neutral grip) and step back slightly so the cable is taut.

Hinge slightly forward at the hips so your torso leans about 10–20 degrees; keep knees soft and maintain a neutral spine. Arms should be extended in front of you with a small, fixed bend in the elbows (not fully locked).

Initiate the rep by driving the shoulders into extension: press the hands downward and back in a controlled arc until the hands reach thigh level and you feel maximal contraction in the lats. Keep the elbows stationary and avoid bending them to pull.
Pause briefly at the bottom while squeezing the lats and maintaining straight-arm tension.
Slowly return the handle to the starting position under control, keeping constant tension on the cable and avoiding letting the shoulders fully relax at the top.
Breathe out during the downward (working) phase and inhale while returning to the start. Perform desired number of reps with steady tempo.
exercises.description: Standing straight-arm cable pushdowns target the lats by driving the arms down from a high cable with minimal elbow flexion. The movement emphasizes shoulder extension and scapular retraction while keeping the torso stable.

exercises.name: Cable Low Fly
exercises.notes: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single-hand handles. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single-hand handles.
Stand centered between the towers in an upright position with feet shoulder-width apart or one foot slightly staggered for stability.
Grasp the handles with a neutral grip so the cables run low behind your legs; step forward slightly to create tension in the cables and keep a tall chest.
Keep a slight bend in the elbows throughout the exercise (soft elbows) and a small forward lean from the hips; do not round the shoulders.
Exhale and pull both handles upward and inward in a controlled wide arc until your hands meet or nearly meet at chest level, squeezing the pectorals at the end of the motion.
Hold the peak contraction briefly, keeping the shoulders down and scapulae stable (use serratus anterior to stabilize the shoulder blades).
Inhale and slowly reverse the movement, allowing the cables to return your arms to the start position with control — do not let the weights slam.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining posture, a consistent elbow angle, and steady tempo throughout.
exercises.description: Standing cable low fly performed with the pulleys set low; you pull each handle upward and inward in a wide arc to bring the hands together in front of the chest, emphasizing horizontal adduction to work the pectorals. Maintain a slight elbow bend and controlled tempo throughout the movement.

exercises.name: Cable Seated Rear Delt Raise
exercises.notes: Set a single handle or dual handles on the low pulley(s) and select a light to moderate weight. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set a single handle or dual handles on the low pulley(s) and select a light to moderate weight.
Sit on the bench facing the pulley, feet flat on the floor and knees slightly bent.
Hinge forward at the hips so your torso is leaning forward (seated, leaning forward position) with a neutral spine and core braced; let your arms hang down toward the pulley.
Grasp the handle(s) with a neutral or thumbs-up grip, elbows slightly bent throughout the movement.

Keeping the chest down and shoulder blades relaxed, raise your upper arms out to the side/diagonal (horizontal abduction) until the elbows reach about shoulder height or just above, leading with the elbows.
Pause briefly at the top and squeeze the rear delts and upper back; avoid shrugging the shoulders toward the ears.
Slowly lower the handle(s) back to the start under control until your arms are fully extended and tension is maintained.
Repeat for the desired reps, maintaining a steady tempo, controlled motion, and neutral spine throughout.
exercises.description: A seated cable variation of the rear delt raise that isolates the posterior deltoid by performing a horizontal abduction/flare of the upper arm while leaning forward. Performed with a low pulley to keep constant tension through the movement.

tips: Every working set should reach true muscular failure.
Use slow, controlled tempo throughout the movement.
Allow full recovery between exercises - this is not cardio.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45° and sit with a dumbbell in each hand resting on your thighs. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45° and sit with a dumbbell in each hand resting on your thighs.
Lie back so your upper back and head are supported (supine on the incline bench) and plant your feet flat on the floor for stability.
Bring the dumbbells up to shoulder level with palms facing forward and elbows bent (upper arms roughly 45° from the torso) while retracting the shoulder blades slightly.
Unrack the dumbbells by pressing them up and then lowering them to start position so the handles are beside your upper chest/shoulder area.
Press the dumbbells upward and slightly together until your arms are nearly straight (avoid locking or hyperextending the elbows); exhale during the press.
Pause briefly at the top with tension in the pectorals, then slowly lower the dumbbells under control back to the upper chest/shoulder level while inhaling.
Keep a stable core and avoid excessive arching of the lower back; maintain scapular control and smooth, controlled reps for the desired number of repetitions.
If using heavy weights, have a spotter or perform a controlled seated dumbbell rack/unrack to avoid strain when starting or finishing the set.
exercises.description: A pressing movement performed lying supine on an incline bench that emphasizes the upper portion of the chest. Dumbbells allow a slightly greater range of motion and independent arm movement compared with a barbell.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with a neutral grip (palms facing your body) and a slight bend in the elbows.
Keep your torso still and shoulders down (do not shrug) throughout the movement.
Exhale and raise both arms out to the sides by leading with the elbows until your arms are approximately parallel to the floor (shoulder height).
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining a slight elbow bend and neutral wrist alignment.
Inhale and lower the dumbbells slowly and controlled back to the starting position at your sides.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, avoiding swinging or leaning the torso to lift heavier weights.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell lateral raises isolate the lateral delts by lifting the arms out to the sides from a seated, back-supported position. Performed with a slight elbow bend and controlled tempo to emphasize the shoulder without using momentum.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Raise
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with arms fully extended down at your sides and palms facing inward; keep a slight bend in the elbows.
Brace your core, keep your chest up and neck neutral, and avoid arching your lower back.
Exhale and lift both dumbbells upward and slightly forward in a controlled arc until your hands reach about shoulder height (arms roughly parallel to the floor).
Pause briefly at the top, maintaining tension in the delts and avoiding momentum or swinging.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting hanging position under control.
Perform the desired repetitions, keeping movement smooth and avoiding use of torso momentum; reduce weight if you need to swing to complete reps.
exercises.description: Seated on an incline bench, this variation of the dumbbell raise emphasizes the front portion of the shoulder (anterior delts) by lifting the weights from a hanging position up to about shoulder height. The incline angle shifts some load onto the chest (pectorals) while isolating the delts and minimizing torso swing.

tips: Every working set should reach true muscular failure.
Use slow, controlled tempo throughout the movement.
Allow full recovery between exercises - this is not cardio.

Grip the bar with hands just outside shoulder width, elbows pointing down and chest up. Stand with feet roughly shoulder-width apart and toes pointed slightly outward.
Brace your core and take a full breath in, then unrack the bar by extending your legs and taking 1–2 controlled steps backward to clear the rack; establish a stable, shoulder-width stance.
Keep a neutral spine and an upright chest. Initiate the movement by pushing your hips back slightly and bending at the knees, sending your knees outward to track over your toes.
Descend in a controlled manner until your thighs are at least parallel to the floor (or deeper if mobility allows) while keeping weight on your midfoot/heels and maintaining a braced core.
Avoid knee collapse (valgus) — consciously push knees outward and keep toes engaged. Do not let the lower back round; maintain a neutral spine throughout.
Drive through your heels and midfoot to stand back up, exhaling as you ascend and extending hips and knees simultaneously until fully upright.

exercises.description: Barbell back squat is a compound, standing lower-body exercise that primarily develops the quadriceps while also engaging the glutes, hamstrings and core for stability. The barbell rests across the upper back while you descend by bending hips and knees, then drive back up to standing.

exercises.name: Barbell Deadlift
exercises.notes: Approach the bar by hinging at the hips and bend the knees until your shins touch the bar; keep hips higher than a squat but lower than full upright. Stop 1 rep short of failure (RPE 9). Spinal safety takes priority.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart with the barbell over the mid-foot and toes pointing slightly out.
Approach the bar by hinging at the hips and bend the knees until your shins touch the bar; keep hips higher than a squat but lower than full upright.
Grip the bar just outside your knees with a double overhand or mixed grip; set shoulders slightly in front of the bar and keep the bar close to your shins.
Brace your core and take a full breath in, keep a neutral (flat) spine and chest lifted — avoid rounding the back.
Drive through the heels and simultaneously extend hips and knees, keeping the bar close to your body and the torso angle changing minimally at first.
Stand tall at the top by finishing hip extension and squeezing the glutes; do not hyperextend the lower back — shoulders should be back but not excessively retracted.
To descend, hinge at the hips first by pushing them back, then bend the knees once the bar passes them, lowering the bar under control to the floor while maintaining a neutral spine.
Reset your breath and core brace at the bottom for the next repetition; maintain consistent foot placement, bar path, and posture throughout the set.
exercises.description: A hip-hinge strength exercise that lifts a loaded barbell from the floor by extending the hips and knees; it targets the posterior chain with heavy involvement of the hamstrings and glutes while the back and core stabilize the torso.

exercises.name: Barbell Seated Calf Raise
exercises.notes: Sit upright near the edge of a flat bench with knees bent at about 90 degrees and feet flat on the floor (or on a small block for increased range of motion). Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Sit upright near the edge of a flat bench with knees bent at about 90 degrees and feet flat on the floor (or on a small block for increased range of motion).
Place a barbell across your upper thighs just above the knees; use a pad if needed for comfort and grip the bar with both hands to steady it (seated position).
Position the balls of your feet on the floor (or a small raised platform) while keeping heels hanging or supported so your ankles can move through full range.
Keeping your torso upright and core engaged, exhale and press through the balls of your feet to raise your heels as high as possible, squeezing the calves at the top.

Pause briefly at the top with the calves fully contracted, then inhale and slowly lower the heels back down until you feel a stretch in the calves but maintain control (avoid letting the weight slam down).
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions; maintain a steady tempo, avoid bouncing, and keep knees stationary throughout the set.
To stop, carefully roll the barbell forward off your thighs or have a spotter help lift it off before standing.
exercises.description: Seated on a bench with a loaded barbell resting across the thighs, raise the heels by plantarflexing the ankles to target the calf muscles. The movement is performed with knees bent, emphasizing the soleus portion of the calves through controlled heel raises and lowers.

tips: Every working set should reach true muscular failure.
Use slow, controlled tempo throughout the movement.
Allow full recovery between exercises - this is not cardio.

exercises.name: Dumbbell One Arm Front Raise
exercises.notes: Stand upright in a neutral stance (feet hip-width), hold a dumbbell in one hand at your side with a firm grip (palm facing down or neutral), and brace your core.
instructions: Stand upright in a neutral stance (feet hip-width), hold a dumbbell in one hand at your side with a firm grip (palm facing down or neutral), and brace your core.
Keep a slight bend in the elbow and maintain a tall chest and neutral spine throughout the movement.
Exhale and lift the dumbbell forward in a controlled arc until your arm is parallel to the floor (shoulder level); avoid leaning back or using momentum.
Pause briefly at the top, keeping the shoulder down (do not shrug) and the elbow slightly bent.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbell back to the starting position with control.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, then switch arms and perform the same number of reps on the opposite side.
Use a lighter weight if you cannot maintain strict form or if you feel shoulder pain; keep core engaged to prevent torso movement.
exercises.description: A unilateral dumbbell front raise performed standing to isolate the anterior delt by lifting the arm straight in front of the body to shoulder height; core and upper-back muscles stabilize the torso. Use a controlled motion with a slight bend in the elbow to protect the joint.

exercises.name: Chin-Up
exercises.notes: Grasp a pull-up bar with a supinated (underhand) grip, hands about shoulder-width apart; hang from the bar with arms fully extended and feet off the ground (dead hang).
instructions: Grasp a pull-up bar with a supinated (underhand) grip, hands about shoulder-width apart; hang from the bar with arms fully extended and feet off the ground (dead hang).
Brace your core and retract your shoulder blades slightly to stabilize the torso before initiating the pull.
Drive your elbows down and back by contracting your biceps and lats, pulling your chest toward the bar.
Continue pulling until your chin clears the bar or the bar touches your upper chest, keeping the torso relatively upright and avoiding excessive swinging.
Pause briefly at the top and squeeze the working muscles (biceps and upper back).
Lower yourself slowly and with control back to the full hang, extending the arms fully while maintaining shoulder blade stability.
Breathe out during the ascent and inhale during the controlled descent.
If you cannot complete a full chin-up, use a band-assisted variation, a box/step for negative-only reps, or an assisted chin-up machine to reduce load while maintaining the same body position.
exercises.description: A vertical pulling exercise performed from a hanging position with a supinated (underhand) grip that primarily loads the biceps while also working the lats and upper back. Pull your chin over the bar and lower under control to build upper-body pulling strength.

exercises.name: Chest Dip
exercises.notes: Starting position: Stand between parallel dip bars or on a dip station and grasp the handles with a firm neutral or slightly pronated grip; support your weight with straight arms and feet off the ground (legs can be bent at the knees or crossed behind you).
instructions: Starting position: Stand between parallel dip bars or on a dip station and grasp the handles with a firm neutral or slightly pronated grip; support your weight with straight arms and feet off the ground (legs can be bent at the knees or crossed behind you).
Adopt a slight forward lean from the hips so your chest moves ahead of your hands; keep your core braced and spine neutral throughout the movement.
Begin the descent by bending at the elbows and lowering your torso in a controlled manner, allowing your elbows to track slightly outward (about 30–45° from the torso) to place emphasis on the chest.
Lower until your shoulders are at or just below elbow level or until you feel a full but comfortable stretch in the chest; avoid excessive depth that causes shoulder pain.
Push back up by driving through the palms and extending the elbows while maintaining the forward lean and chest-up posture; exhale as you press upward.
Return to the start with arms nearly straight but avoid aggressively locking the elbows at the top; maintain control and avoid swinging the body.
Perform repetitions with a steady tempo (e.g., 2–3 seconds down, 1 second pause, 1 second up) and focus on chest contraction each rep.

Progression and safety: if bodyweight is too difficult use assisted dip machine or band assistance; if you want more chest emphasis increase forward lean slightly. Stop if you experience sharp shoulder pain.
exercises.description: A bodyweight dip performed on parallel bars with a forward lean to emphasize the chest; the movement lowers the torso by bending the elbows then pushes the body back up to full arm extension. It targets the pectorals while the triceps and anterior delts assist and stabilize.

exercises.name: Pull-up (neutral grip)
exercises.notes: Stand under the pull-up handles and reach up to grasp the parallel (neutral) grips with palms facing each other, hands about shoulder-width apart.
instructions: Stand under the pull-up handles and reach up to grasp the parallel (neutral) grips with palms facing each other, hands about shoulder-width apart.
Hang fully with arms extended, shoulders relaxed but slightly depressed and scapulae engaged (avoid hanging completely passive); cross or bend your legs to reduce swinging if needed.
Brace your core and initiate the movement by pulling your shoulder blades down and together, then drive your elbows down toward your ribs while pulling your chest up toward the handles.
Continue pulling until your chin clears the level of your hands or your chest reaches the handles, keeping the torso relatively upright and avoiding excessive kipping.
Pause briefly at the top with shoulders engaged, then lower yourself slowly and under control back to the full-hang position with arms extended.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining smooth controlled reps and avoiding momentum or excessive body swing.
exercises.description: A neutral-grip pull-up performed hanging from parallel handles with palms facing each other; you pull the torso upward until the chin clears the hands, emphasizing vertical pulling with strong lat engagement. This variation increases biceps involvement and is slightly easier on the shoulders than a wide pronated grip.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Front Raise
exercises.notes: Stand upright with feet about shoulder-width apart, knees soft, and core braced (standing position).
instructions: Stand upright with feet about shoulder-width apart, knees soft, and core braced (standing position).
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral or overhand grip and the weights resting in front of your thighs.
Keep a slight bend in your elbows throughout the movement to protect the joint and avoid locking.
Exhale and raise both dumbbells straight in front of you to roughly shoulder height, leading with the hands and keeping the wrists neutral.
Pause for a second at the top while maintaining upright posture and a tight core; do not lean backward or use hip drive.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the start position under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, keeping movement slow and controlled to minimize momentum and maximize delt activation.
If balance or form breaks down, reduce the weight or perform the raise one arm at a time.
exercises.description: A standing dumbbell front raise variation where both dumbbells are lifted together in front of the body to work the anterior shoulder. Performed with a slight bend in the elbow and controlled tempo to isolate the delts and minimize momentum.

tips: Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.
Focus on breathing patterns - exhale on exertion.
Keep transitions between exercises quick.

exercises.name: Cable One Arm Front Raise
exercises.notes: Attach a single handle to the low pulley and select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Attach a single handle to the low pulley and select an appropriate weight.

Stand upright facing forward a short distance from the machine with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart; hold the handle in one hand with a pronated (palm down) or neutral grip and let the arm hang at your side.
Step slightly away from the machine so there is light tension on the cable; brace your core, keep a tall chest, and maintain a slight bend in the elbow throughout.
Keeping the torso still and shoulders down, raise the arm straight forward in a controlled motion until the hand reaches about shoulder height (parallel to the floor), leading with the elbow.
Pause briefly (about 1 second) at the top while maintaining scapular stability and avoiding an excessive shrug.
Slowly lower the arm back to the starting position under control until the hand returns to your thigh and tension is maintained.
Breathe out during the lift and inhale as you lower; avoid swinging the body, using momentum, or leaning backward to lift the weight.
Complete the desired repetitions, then switch sides and repeat with the other arm.
exercises.description: A unilateral cable front raise performed from a low pulley to isolate the anterior deltoid by lifting the arm straight in front of the body to shoulder height. The cable provides constant tension through the movement and requires core stabilization to prevent torso lean.

exercises.name: Cable Standing Chest Press
exercises.notes: Set both cable pulleys to about chest/shoulder height and attach single hand grips; select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to about chest/shoulder height and attach single hand grips; select an appropriate weight.

Stand in the middle of the station facing away from the stacks with one foot slightly forward (staggered stance) for stability, arms bent and handles held at chest level, elbows roughly at chest height.
Brace your core (engage abs), keep a neutral spine and shoulders slightly retracted to set the starting position; wrists remain neutral.
Step forward or press your feet into the floor to create tension in the cables so the handles cannot move without effort.
Exhale and press both handles forward in a controlled motion, extending the arms until nearly straight but without locking the elbows; keep the movement horizontal and drive from the chest.
At full extension, pause briefly and squeeze the pectorals (and serratus anterior) while keeping the torso stable and not overextending the lower back.
Inhale and slowly return the handles to the start position with control, allowing the elbows to bend to chest level while maintaining core tension.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, keeping consistent tempo and avoiding excessive leaning or rotating of the torso.
exercises.description: A standing cable press performed from a dual‑pulley station that emphasizes horizontal pushing to work the chest. The movement also requires shoulder and triceps involvement while the core stabilizes the torso.

exercises.name: Cable Straight Back Seated Row
exercises.notes: Set your spine in a neutral (straight) position with chest up and shoulders relaxed—avoid rounding the back or excessive leaning.
instructions: Sit on the bench with feet braced against the footplate and knees slightly bent; grasp the close/V-handle with both hands and sit tall.
Set your spine in a neutral (straight) position with chest up and shoulders relaxed—avoid rounding the back or excessive leaning.
Begin the repetition by pulling your shoulder blades back and down (scapular retraction) to initiate the movement.
Continue pulling the handle toward your lower chest/upper abdomen by driving your elbows straight back; keep elbows close to the body.
Squeeze the lats and upper back at the end range for 1 second while maintaining an upright seated position (do not hyperextend the lower back).
Return the handle slowly under control by extending the arms and allowing the scapulae to protract until arms are nearly straight.
Breathe out as you pull, inhale as you return; use a controlled tempo and avoid using torso momentum to complete the reps.
exercises.description: Seated cable row performed with a straight, neutral spine; pull the handle toward the torso using shoulder blade retraction to emphasize the lats and upper back. Maintain an upright torso and controlled tempo throughout the movement.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Raise
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with arms fully extended down at your sides and palms facing inward; keep a slight bend in the elbows.
Brace your core, keep your chest up and neck neutral, and avoid arching your lower back.
Exhale and lift both dumbbells upward and slightly forward in a controlled arc until your hands reach about shoulder height (arms roughly parallel to the floor).
Pause briefly at the top, maintaining tension in the delts and avoiding momentum or swinging.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting hanging position under control.
Perform the desired repetitions, keeping movement smooth and avoiding use of torso momentum; reduce weight if you need to swing to complete reps.
exercises.description: Seated on an incline bench, this variation of the dumbbell raise emphasizes the front portion of the shoulder (anterior delts) by lifting the weights from a hanging position up to about shoulder height. The incline angle shifts some load onto the chest (pectorals) while isolating the delts and minimizing torso swing.

tips: Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.
Focus on breathing patterns - exhale on exertion.
Keep transitions between exercises quick.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Alternative Fly
exercises.notes: Set an adjustable bench to a 30–45° incline and sit with a dumbbell in each hand.
instructions: Set an adjustable bench to a 30–45° incline and sit with a dumbbell in each hand. Plant your feet flat on the floor and lie back so your head, upper back, and butt are supported (supine on incline bench).
Hold the dumbbells with a neutral or slightly pronated grip and press them together over your upper chest with a small bend in both elbows; this is the start position.
Keep your shoulder blades lightly retracted and braced throughout the set to protect the shoulder joint.

Lower one dumbbell in a slow, controlled arc out to the side (maintain the same elbow bend) until you feel a stretch in the upper chest; keep the opposite arm stationary and slightly elevated above the chest.
Pause briefly at the bottom for tension on the pectorals, then drive that dumbbell back up in the same arc to meet the elevated dumbbell above the chest, squeezing the pectorals at the top.
Alternate sides each repetition, maintaining control, a constant elbow angle, and avoiding bouncing or letting the lowered elbow drop behind the plane of your torso.
Breathe in as you lower the weight and exhale as you bring it back up. Use a weight that allows strict form and full control for the full range of motion.
Avoid shrugging the shoulders or overarching the lower back; stop immediately if you feel sharp shoulder pain and reduce range or load.
exercises.description: Lying supine on an incline bench, perform alternating single-arm dumbbell flyes in a wide arc to isolate the upper chest (pectorals) while one arm remains elevated as the other lowers. The movement emphasizes a controlled stretch and contraction of the upper chest with stabilization from the anterior delts and forearms.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Alternate Shoulder Press
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet about hip-width apart, knees slightly bent and core (abs) braced to keep the torso stable.
instructions: Stand tall with feet about hip-width apart, knees slightly bent and core (abs) braced to keep the torso stable.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height with palms facing forward, elbows bent and aligned slightly in front of the body.
Press one dumbbell straight overhead by extending the elbow and driving through the shoulder until the arm is nearly straight (avoid locking the elbow); exhale during the press.
Lower that dumbbell under control back to shoulder height while inhaling.
Immediately press the other dumbbell overhead in the same controlled manner, alternating arms with each repetition.
Keep the ribcage down and avoid excessive arching of the lower back; use the traps to stabilize the shoulders and maintain a neutral head position throughout.
Perform the movement in a slow, controlled rhythm and choose a weight that allows full range of motion without compensatory torso lean.
exercises.description: A standing alternating dumbbell shoulder press where one dumbbell is pressed overhead at a time; primarily develops the delts while the triceps assist and the traps and core stabilize the torso.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Reverse Grip Incline Bench Row
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and position it so your chest rests firmly on the pad with feet braced on the floor or foot supports (prone position).
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and position it so your chest rests firmly on the pad with feet braced on the floor or foot supports (prone position).
Load a barbell and grasp it with a shoulder-width reverse (underhand) grip so palms face up and arms hang straight down toward the floor.
Start with your chest pressed into the bench, shoulders relaxed and scapulae slightly protracted, core engaged and neck neutral.

Initiate the movement by retracting your scapulae and pulling the barbell upward in a controlled path toward your lower chest / upper abdomen, leading with the elbows and keeping them close to your sides.
Pause 1 second at the top and squeeze the lats and upper back without hyperextending the spine or lifting your chest off the bench.
Lower the barbell slowly back to the start with full arm extension while maintaining tension in the lats; avoid dropping the weight or using momentum.
Repeat for the desired reps. Breathe out while pulling and inhale while lowering.
exercises.description: Prone incline bench row performed with a reverse (underhand) grip that emphasizes the lats while also engaging the upper back and arms. Lying chest-down on an incline bench, you pull a barbell up toward the lower chest/upper abdomen and lower it under control.

exercises.name: Lever T-bar Row (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Stand on the machine foot platforms with feet hip-width apart and straddle the bar, grasping the handles with a neutral or overhand grip.
instructions: Stand on the machine foot platforms with feet hip-width apart and straddle the bar, grasping the handles with a neutral or overhand grip.
Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is inclined forward (maintain a neutral spine and avoid rounding), and brace your core.
Begin with your arms extended and shoulders protracted so the weight is under control.
Exhale and pull the handles toward your lower chest/upper abdomen by driving your elbows back and squeezing your shoulder blades together.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining scapular retraction and keeping the torso stable (avoid excessive torso lean or momentum).
Inhale and slowly lower the handles back to the start under control until arms are nearly extended, keeping tension in the back throughout.
Repeat for the desired reps, keeping movements smooth and avoiding shrugging the shoulders or rounding the lower back.
Adjust foot position or torso angle as needed to maintain a comfortable, neutral spine and full range of motion.
exercises.description: A bent-over plate-loaded T-bar row where you stand on the foot platform and hinge at the hips to pull the handle toward your torso, primarily working the lats and mid-back. The movement emphasizes scapular retraction and elbow drive while maintaining a neutral spine.

exercises.name: Chin-Up
exercises.notes: Grasp a pull-up bar with a supinated (underhand) grip, hands about shoulder-width apart; hang from the bar with arms fully extended and feet off the ground (dead hang).
instructions: Grasp a pull-up bar with a supinated (underhand) grip, hands about shoulder-width apart; hang from the bar with arms fully extended and feet off the ground (dead hang).
Brace your core and retract your shoulder blades slightly to stabilize the torso before initiating the pull.
Drive your elbows down and back by contracting your biceps and lats, pulling your chest toward the bar.
Continue pulling until your chin clears the bar or the bar touches your upper chest, keeping the torso relatively upright and avoiding excessive swinging.
Pause briefly at the top and squeeze the working muscles (biceps and upper back).
Lower yourself slowly and with control back to the full hang, extending the arms fully while maintaining shoulder blade stability.
Breathe out during the ascent and inhale during the controlled descent.
If you cannot complete a full chin-up, use a band-assisted variation, a box/step for negative-only reps, or an assisted chin-up machine to reduce load while maintaining the same body position.
exercises.description: A vertical pulling exercise performed from a hanging position with a supinated (underhand) grip that primarily loads the biceps while also working the lats and upper back. Pull your chin over the bar and lower under control to build upper-body pulling strength.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.
